name: Crime
description: Stories focused on criminal acts and their consequences, often emphasizing investigation, moral compromise, power, and social order.

nodes.label: Crime Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story that explores criminal acts and their consequences — a world where the line between law and morality is contested, power determines who survives, and no one escapes without cost.
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
failure_modes: Claims to be crime but treats criminality as background decoration
Criminal acts have no meaningful consequences
Moral stakes are absent
signals_in_text: Opening establishes a criminal world or a criminal act as the central subject
Tone signals that law and morality are under pressure
Reader/viewer understands this world operates by different rules

nodes.label: Criminal Activity Must Be Central
nodes.definition: The criminal act — its planning, execution, discovery, or aftermath — must be the primary engine of the narrative. Crime cannot be incidental or backdrop; it must be the subject.
exit_conditions: Every major plot development connects to the central criminal activity
typical_variants: Crime in progress as the story's core
Criminal lifestyle as the narrative frame
Investigation of crime as central engine
failure_modes: Crime is a convenient plot device for a story really about something else
Criminal acts are episodic and unconnected
Story pivots away from the crime to purely personal drama
signals_in_text: Scenes revolve around criminal acts, their planning, or their fallout
Characters are defined by their relationship to the crime
Narrative momentum driven by criminal activity or its detection

nodes.label: Consequences Must Be Real and Proportional
nodes.definition: Crime must carry genuine cost — physical, psychological, social, or moral. The crime cannot be committed without something being damaged or destroyed, and consequences must be proportional to the act.
exit_conditions: At least one significant consequence of the criminal act drives the narrative
typical_variants: Characters pay with their relationships or identity
Collateral damage to innocents creates moral weight
failure_modes: Crime is glamorous and costless
Consequences are avoided through convenience or authorial protection
Stakes are announced but never actually delivered
signals_in_text: Characters suffer loss or diminishment as a direct result of criminal activity
The criminal act damages something that cannot be fully repaired
Secondary characters bear the cost of protagonist's choices

nodes.label: Moral Ambiguity — The Line Is Blurred
nodes.definition: The crime story must complicate the boundary between criminal and legitimate — heroes may commit crimes, criminals may be sympathetic, and institutions of law may themselves be corrupt. Simple moral binaries are refused.
exit_conditions: At least one major moral position in the story is genuinely contested or compromised
typical_variants: Legitimate institutions built on criminal foundations
failure_modes: Story relies on simple good vs. evil without complication
Criminal characters are cartoonishly evil with no redeeming complexity
The law is always right and always wins without cost
signals_in_text: Audience sympathizes with characters doing wrong
Authority figures are shown to be compromised
Characters justify their crimes with internally coherent logic
The legitimate world is shown to be built on the same mechanisms as the criminal one

nodes.label: Power Dynamics Drive the Conflict
nodes.definition: Crime stories are fundamentally about who has power, how it is acquired, maintained, and lost. The central conflict must be expressed through power dynamics — control of territory, loyalty, information, money, or institutional authority.
exit_conditions: Power relationships between major characters shape every key conflict
typical_variants: Criminal vs. law enforcement power contest
Rise and fall of a powerful criminal figure
Underdog challenger vs. established criminal empire
failure_modes: Conflict is personal without structural power dimension
Power dynamics are ignored in favor of pure action
Characters have equivalent power with no meaningful hierarchy
signals_in_text: Characters maneuver for position and advantage
Decisions are made based on loyalty, leverage, or control
Scenes establish who gives orders and who obeys
Power shifts are felt as the story's major turning points

nodes.label: Organized Crime / Mob Story
nodes.definition: Stories centered on institutionalized criminal enterprise — the mob, cartel, gang, or syndicate — where loyalty, hierarchy, and the internal codes of the organization drive the narrative as much as external threat.
entry_conditions: Criminal activity is organized, hierarchical, and institutional
exit_conditions: The organization's internal logic and codes shape all major conflicts
failure_modes: Organization is present but its internal logic is irrelevant to the plot
Loyalty and betrayal are not meaningful stakes
Hierarchy exists only as set dressing
signals_in_text: Characters navigate internal hierarchy as much as external threat
Loyalty and betrayal are central moral currencies
The organization's rules and codes are treated as law
Characters define themselves through their rank and affiliation

nodes.label: Heist / Caper
nodes.definition: Stories structured around the planning and execution of a specific criminal act — a robbery, con, or scheme — where the mechanics of the crime, the team dynamic, and the inevitable complications are the primary sources of tension and pleasure.
entry_conditions: A specific criminal act is planned and executed as the narrative spine
exit_conditions: The story follows the full arc of conception, assembly, planning, execution, and aftermath of the targeted crime
failure_modes: Planning phase is skipped — execution lacks earned tension
Team dynamic is irrelevant to success or failure
Complications during execution are random rather than arising from established character or plan flaws
Aftermath has no consequences
signals_in_text: Detailed planning sequences that establish what must go right
Ensemble of specialists with distinct roles
Execution scenes where established plans deviate
Complications arise from character flaws or overlooked variables

nodes.label: Crime Thriller / Noir
nodes.definition: Stories characterized by atmosphere, moral decay, and fatalism — where the criminal world corrupts everything it touches, the protagonist is complicit in their own destruction, and the system offers no clean escape.
entry_conditions: Atmosphere of moral decay and fatalism is central to the story's identity
exit_conditions: The tone of inevitability and corruption permeates scene construction, character arc, and resolution
failure_modes: Atmosphere is surface-level without structural fatalism
Protagonist escapes cleanly without moral cost
World is corrupt but the story treats this as background rather than subject
signals_in_text: Narration or dialogue reflects a world-weary, disillusioned worldview
Settings — rain-soaked streets, dim interiors, decaying institutions — mirror the moral state
Protagonist is drawn further into the criminal world despite resistance
Resolution is ambiguous or pyrrhic

nodes.label: True Crime / Docudrama
nodes.definition: Stories grounded in real criminal events, treating crime through an investigative or institutional lens — reconstructing what happened, why it happened, and how institutions (law enforcement, media, courts) responded.
entry_conditions: Story is explicitly based on real events or adopts the aesthetic and structural conventions of the true crime form
exit_conditions: Narrative maintains fidelity to documentary logic — evidence, testimony, institutional process, and unanswered questions
typical_variants: Courtroom procedural based on real case
failure_modes: Fictionalization overrides documentary integrity
Institutional process is simplified to action or melodrama
Victims are reduced to plot devices
Ambiguity is artificially resolved for dramatic satisfaction
signals_in_text: Explicit reference to real events, names, or records
Documentary techniques — archival footage, testimony framing, timeline reconstruction
Institutional process given serious weight
Unresolved questions are acknowledged rather than papered over

nodes.label: Criminal Ecosystem — The Underworld Has Its Own Rules
nodes.definition: The criminal world must be rendered as a functioning system with its own economy, hierarchy, code of conduct, and enforcement mechanisms. The underworld is not chaos — it has structure, and violating its rules has consequences.
exit_conditions: The criminal world's internal logic governs character behavior and plot consequences
typical_variants: Mob omertà and code of silence
Con artist community with its own etiquette
Criminal market with prices and competition
failure_modes: Criminal world operates on random violence with no internal logic
Rules of the underworld are never established or enforced
Characters violate the code without consequence
signals_in_text: Characters reference rules, codes, and expectations of the criminal world
Violations of the code trigger predictable consequences
Economic logic of the criminal enterprise is visible
Characters navigate the underworld as a known social system

nodes.label: Law Enforcement Presence — Justice Is Visible and Active
nodes.definition: The system of law — police, courts, prosecutors, investigators — must be a visible and active force in the story. Even when corrupt or ineffective, the law's presence shapes the criminal world and creates pressure on all parties.
exit_conditions: Law enforcement's activity influences at least one major plot turn or character decision
typical_variants: Active police investigation closing in on protagonists
Corrupt officers on the criminal payroll
Prosecutor building a case over the course of the story
failure_modes: Law enforcement is entirely absent from a crime story
Police are present but have no effect on events
Justice system is mentioned but never functions as a meaningful pressure
signals_in_text: Scenes depict police procedure, surveillance, or investigation
Characters make decisions based on law enforcement presence or likelihood of detection
Law enforcement figures have their own arc and motivations
Legal consequences loom over criminal activity

nodes.label: Territory and Power — Physical Spaces Represent Control
nodes.definition: Physical spaces in crime stories — neighborhoods, buildings, borders, corners — must carry the meaning of power and control. Who controls a space, who is welcome, and who is excluded are expressions of the underlying power dynamics.
exit_conditions: Physical settings are rendered as contested or controlled spaces that reflect the story's power relationships
typical_variants: Criminal organization's home turf vs. rivals
Safe houses and controlled spaces as power assets
Neutral ground where different factions meet
The legitimate city as backdrop to the criminal geography underneath it
failure_modes: Settings are interchangeable — no sense of territory or control
Physical space has no relationship to the power dynamics
Characters move freely through spaces that should be dangerous or forbidden
signals_in_text: Characters reference who owns or controls particular areas
Movement between spaces requires permission, negotiation, or risk
Settings are described in terms of who belongs there
Violation of territorial boundaries triggers conflict

nodes.label: The Crime Scene — The Central Criminal Act or Its Discovery
nodes.definition: A scene — or sequence of scenes — that establishes the central criminal act in full: either its planning and commission, or its discovery and aftermath. This scene anchors the story's stakes and makes the crime concrete and real.
entry_conditions: Protagonist, setting, and criminal ecosystem established
exit_conditions: The central crime is rendered concrete — audience understands what was done, to whom, and at what cost
typical_variants: Discovery of a body or evidence of crime
Surveillance footage or testimony reconstructing the act
The moment the protagonist crosses into criminal activity
The aftermath scene establishing what was taken or destroyed
failure_modes: Crime is described but never shown — stakes remain abstract
The criminal act is ambiguous to the point of irrelevance
Crime is established but its human cost is invisible
signals_in_text: Scene is given significant narrative weight and detail
Audience can picture the crime and its immediate consequences
Emotional and material stakes are established concretely
The crime's specific nature shapes all subsequent events

nodes.label: The Moral Compromise Scene — Character Crosses a Line
nodes.definition: A scene in which a character makes a choice that irrevocably compromises their moral standing — they cross a line they cannot uncross, either by committing a crime, enabling one, or sacrificing a principle for survival or gain.
entry_conditions: Pressure or temptation brings them to the threshold
exit_conditions: Character has made a choice with real moral cost, and the story acknowledges it
typical_variants: First criminal act — the moment of initiation
Betrayal of a loyal partner for self-preservation
Acceptance of dirty money or corrupt help
Order to commit violence that character carries out
Decision to look away from an injustice for personal benefit
failure_modes: The moral compromise is undone or forgiven too quickly
Character shows no awareness that a line has been crossed
The scene is played as morally neutral or heroic without complication
signals_in_text: Character hesitates before choosing
Dialogue or inner monologue acknowledges the weight of the decision
The choice is not easily reversible
Other characters or the narrative register the moral significance
Character's relationship to themselves shifts after the scene

nodes.label: The Reckoning Scene — Consequences Arrive
nodes.definition: A scene — often late in the narrative — in which the consequences of the central crime and the accumulated moral compromises arrive in full force. This is the payoff of the genre's promise that crime has a cost.
entry_conditions: Criminal acts and moral compromises have been committed and accrued
exit_conditions: Consequences are delivered in proportion to the story's accumulated weight — arrest, violence, loss, destruction, or moral ruin
typical_variants: Violent retribution from enemies or partners
Loss of everything built through criminal activity
Protagonist confronts what they have become
The moment of institutional exposure or public ruin
failure_modes: Consequences are avoided or deflected at the last moment
Reckoning is disproportionately mild relative to the crimes committed
Resolution is too clean — character escapes without meaningful cost
Reckoning is external (arrest) without internal (moral) dimension
signals_in_text: The scene has the weight of accumulated story — previous events make this moment inevitable
Character cannot escape or bargain their way out
Loss or damage is concrete and permanent
Tone signals finality — the world has changed and will not be restored

nodes.label: Moral Weight and Gritty Realism
nodes.definition: Crime stories require a tonal commitment to the real weight of criminal activity — the violence is not stylized into fun, the moral compromises are not trivialized, and the human cost of crime is not elided. Gritty realism means the world behaves as the world does.
exit_conditions: Tone maintains gravity throughout — even moments of levity do not undercut the story's fundamental seriousness about crime and consequence
typical_variants: Institutional realism — the law works slowly, imperfectly, corruptly
Emotional truth in criminal relationships — loyalty, betrayal, grief
failure_modes: Violence is consequence-free spectacle
Criminal lifestyle is rendered as pure aspiration
Moral complexity is resolved too neatly
The real human cost of crime — to victims, families, communities — is invisible
signals_in_text: Scenes of violence carry emotional aftermath
Characters show the psychological cost of their choices
Institutional processes are rendered with specificity and friction
Victims are rendered as full human beings, not plot devices

nodes.label: Glamorized Crime Without Consequences
nodes.definition: Crime story fails when it presents criminal activity as straightforwardly aspirational — the lifestyle is glamorous, the violence is cool, and the consequences are minimal or absent. This collapses the genre's moral architecture.
exit_conditions: Criminal activity is rendered with genuine moral weight and proportional consequences
typical_variants: Heist presented as pure fun with no one hurt
Criminal lifestyle as power fantasy without cost
Protagonist achieves wealth and status through crime with no meaningful downside
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience is invited to admire the criminal lifestyle without complication
Violence is exciting but not disturbing
Characters accumulate rewards of crime without corresponding loss
Victims are invisible or treated as deserving
Ending rewards criminal success

edges.label: Promise requires crime to be central
edges.effects_on_stakes: All major narrative stakes must be rooted in criminal activity
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are defined by their relationship to the central criminal act
common_alternatives: Crime as the fulcrum of all plot developments vs. crime as one strand among several
anti_patterns: Crime as backdrop to a story primarily about something else

edges.label: Promise requires real consequences
edges.effects_on_stakes: Crime must damage something real — freedom, relationship, life, or soul
edges.effects_on_character: No character commits crime without bearing some cost
common_alternatives: Consequences arrive immediately vs. accumulate over time vs. arrive as final reckoning
anti_patterns: Criminal acts that are costless or easily undone

edges.label: Promise requires moral ambiguity
edges.effects_on_stakes: The audience cannot simply root for law or criminal — both are compromised
edges.effects_on_character: Characters resist easy moral categorization
common_alternatives: Ambiguity located in protagonist vs. antagonist vs. institution vs. all three
anti_patterns: Clear moral binary where the right side wins cleanly

edges.label: Promise requires power dynamics
edges.effects_on_stakes: Conflict is always an expression of who holds power and who is seeking it
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' choices are shaped by their position in the power hierarchy
common_alternatives: Power through violence vs. money vs. information vs. institutional authority
anti_patterns: Conflict that is purely personal without structural power dimension

edges.label: Promise requires moral weight and gritty realism
edges.effects_on_stakes: Even moments of excitement or humor are underlaid by the story's fundamental seriousness
edges.effects_on_character: Characters inhabit a world where actions have weight
common_alternatives: Gritty vs. stylized realism — both require moral weight even if visual register differs
anti_patterns: Criminal world rendered as pure entertainment spectacle without gravity

edges.label: Power dynamics institutionalized in criminal hierarchy
preconditions: Power dynamics are expressed through a hierarchical criminal organization
edges.effects_on_stakes: Loyalty and betrayal within the organization are matters of life and death
edges.effects_on_character: Character's identity and survival are tied to their place in the hierarchy
anti_patterns: Organization exists but its hierarchy has no bearing on the story

edges.label: Criminal act is the planned and executed subject
preconditions: Criminal activity is a specific, planned act that structures the entire narrative
edges.effects_on_stakes: Success or failure of the heist is the story's central dramatic question
edges.effects_on_character: Each team member's role and personal agenda shapes the plan and its complications
anti_patterns: Crime is improvised and unplanned — the heist structure requires deliberate architecture

edges.label: Moral ambiguity taken to fatalistic extreme
preconditions: Moral ambiguity is so pervasive that the world itself is corrupt — there is no clean ground to stand on
edges.effects_on_stakes: No escape from the corruption — even the attempt to do right is tainted
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is complicit in the world they are trying to navigate or escape
anti_patterns: Noir atmosphere without fatalistic structure — protagonist escapes cleanly

edges.label: Consequences examined through documentary and institutional lens
preconditions: Real events ground the consequences in documented, verifiable fact
edges.effects_on_stakes: The story's moral weight is reinforced by its factual basis — these things actually happened
edges.effects_on_character: Real people's actions and institutional failures are placed under scrutiny
anti_patterns: True crime aesthetic without documentary integrity — fictionalization that obscures rather than illuminates

edges.label: Organized crime requires a functioning criminal ecosystem
edges.effects_on_stakes: The organization's rules and economy create the pressure that drives all conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Characters live inside the ecosystem — its logic is their logic
common_alternatives: Mob hierarchy vs. cartel supply chain vs. gang territorial system
anti_patterns: Criminal organization with no internal logic or rules

edges.label: Heist requires mastery and control of physical space
edges.effects_on_stakes: The target location is a controlled space that must be understood, penetrated, and escaped
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must map, plan, and navigate a space designed to exclude them
anti_patterns: Physical space of the heist target is irrelevant to planning and execution

edges.label: Noir requires a world saturated by criminal logic
edges.effects_on_stakes: The criminal ecosystem is not just the underworld — it permeates the legitimate world too
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist cannot stand outside the ecosystem — they are part of it
anti_patterns: Noir world where the protagonist can find clean ground to operate from

edges.label: True crime requires serious institutional law enforcement presence
edges.effects_on_stakes: Institutional response — its speed, competence, and integrity — is itself under examination
edges.effects_on_character: Law enforcement figures carry the weight of systemic success or failure
anti_patterns: True crime story where law enforcement is invisible or irrelevant

edges.label: Criminal ecosystem makes the crime scene legible
edges.effects_on_stakes: The crime scene is understood within the rules and logic of the criminal world
edges.effects_on_character: Characters read the crime scene through the lens of their knowledge of the ecosystem
common_alternatives: Insider perspective on crime scene vs. outsider investigative perspective
anti_patterns: Crime scene exists in isolation from the world that produced it

edges.label: Law enforcement presence makes legal reckoning possible
preconditions: Law enforcement has been actively building toward accountability
edges.effects_on_stakes: Reckoning may arrive through legal channels — arrest, trial, conviction
edges.effects_on_character: The law's arrival is experienced as consequence, not rescue
anti_patterns: Law enforcement is present throughout but plays no role in the reckoning

edges.label: Control of space demands moral compromise to maintain
edges.effects_on_stakes: Holding or taking power requires crossing moral lines
edges.effects_on_character: Character must compromise their values to compete in the power landscape
anti_patterns: Character accumulates power and territory without any moral cost

edges.label: The crime scene is the seed of the reckoning
preconditions: Central crime has been established in concrete detail
edges.effects_on_stakes: The reckoning's weight is proportional to how real the crime was made to feel
edges.effects_on_character: What was done in the crime scene must be answered for in the reckoning scene
common_alternatives: Delayed consequence — crime catches up over time
anti_patterns: Crime scene is detailed and concrete but the reckoning is vague or avoided

edges.label: Moral compromise must be paid for at the reckoning
preconditions: Moral compromise has been committed and registered by the narrative
edges.effects_on_stakes: The reckoning must account for what the character became, not just what they did
edges.effects_on_character: Internal reckoning — who the character is now — accompanies external consequence
anti_patterns: Character undergoes serious moral compromise but faces no meaningful internal or external reckoning

edges.label: Moral compromise often occurs at or because of the crime scene
preconditions: Character is present at or implicated in the central criminal act
edges.effects_on_stakes: The crime scene is also the site of personal moral failure, not just external event
edges.effects_on_character: Character's direct participation in the crime is what makes the compromise concrete
common_alternatives: Moral compromise occurs before the crime — enabling it
Moral compromise occurs after — covering it up
Moral compromise occurs at the scene — committing it
anti_patterns: Moral compromise is entirely disconnected from the central criminal act

edges.label: Absent consequences produce glamorized crime
preconditions: Criminal activity is depicted without proportional consequence
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes collapse — crime becomes aspiration rather than moral subject
edges.effects_on_character: Criminal characters become power fantasies rather than complex figures
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid when consequences fail
